elements.sent-2411-018: Despite the difficulties due to large variations of the light curves of active stars, many candidate exoplanets have been found both around members of young stellar clusters, associations, and moving groups (e.g., \citealt{2020AJ....160..239B,2020AJ....160...33R,2021AJ....161...65N,2020AJ....160..179M,2020MNRAS.498.5972N,2021MNRAS.505.3767N,2021AJ....161..171T, 2022AJ....163..156M,2023AJ....165...85W, 2024AJ....168...41T ) and orbiting young single stars (e.g., \citealt{2022ApJ...935L..10G,2022AJ....163...99G,2022AJ....164...71V,2022MNRAS.516.4432M ).}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The intensive spectroscopic follow-ups of these objects have allowed not only to put constraints on the planetary masses (e.g., \citealt{2019A A...630A..81B,benatti2021,2022MNRAS.514.1606B,2022MNRAS.513.5955K,2023A A...675A.158D,2023A A...672A.126D,2024MNRAS.531.4275B,2024A A...688A..15D,2023arXiv231016888M,2024A A...682A.135C ) but also to identify extended (escaping) atmospheres (e.g., \citealt{orellmiquel2023,zhang2023,2023MNRAS.518.3777G,2024AJ....167..214P,2024arXiv240609225M ).}}

elements.sent-2411-021: The Global Architecture of Planetary Systems (GAPS) collaboration (\citealt{2013A A...554A..28C ) is aimed at spectroscopic follow-up of transiting and non-transiting exoplanets with the goal of determining planetary masses, as well as measuring orbital properties (\citealt{2015A A...581L...6D ), detecting planetary atmospheres (e.g., \citealt{2022A A...668A.176P,2024A A...687A.143S,2024A A...686A..83G ) and characterizing the host stars (\citealt{2022A A...663A.142M,2022A A...664A.161B,2024A A...682A.136C ).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-065: With this method, the observed spectrum is cross-correlated with a binary mask depicting the typical features of a specific spectral type to obtain a normalized weighted mean of the line profiles of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the case of TOI-1430, we used a K5 mask\footnote{Although the K2 mask would be the best choice for this star, it is currently not available in the YABI interface.}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, we do not expect this choice to affect the RV precision, which is limited by stellar activity.. .

elements.sent-2411-076: All HIRES observations were obtained with a warm (50\degree C) cell of molecular iodine at the entrance slit \citep{butler96 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: The superposition of the iodine absorption lines on the stellar spectrum provides both a fiducial wavelength solution and a precise, observation-specific characterization of the instrument's PSF.
elements.sent-2411-078: The mean airmass of the observations is $1.5 \pm 0.4$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Across all 113 HIRES iodine-in spectra, the observations have a median exposure length of 305 s and a median \snr of 220 per pixel at 5500 \AA.
elements.sent-2411-080: The majority of the observations (99 out of 113) were taken using the B5 decker (3\farcs5 $\times$ 0\farcs861, $R=$ 45,000), while the remaining exposures used the C2 decker (14\arcsec $\times$ 0\farcs861, $R=$ 45,000).
elements.sent-2411-081: The only difference between the two is that the length of the C2 decker enables better sky subtraction during data reduction, though this is not critical for a bright star like TOI-1430 (choosing B5 versus C2 typically makes a difference for targets with $V > 10$ mag).
elements.sent-2411-082: The exposures were taken with a median seeing of 1\farcs2 and all observations were collected with a moon separation of $>30$\degree.
elements.sent-2411-083: To measure precise RVs using the iodine method, a high-resolution, high-\snr, iodine-free ``template'' spectrum must also be obtained to create a deconvolved stellar spectral template (DSST) of the host star.
elements.sent-2411-084: To compute RVs, we used the iodine-free template of TOI-1430 taken by TKS on 10 December 2019.
elements.sent-2411-085: The template was produced by averaging together two iodine-free exposures taken back-to-back.
elements.sent-2411-086: Each spectrum was collected using the B3 decker (14\arcsec $\times$ 0\farcs574, $R=$ 60,000), which provides slightly higher resolution compared to the B5 or C2 deckers at the cost of throughput.
elements.sent-2411-087: The spectra were obtained at an airmass of 2.6, in seeing of 1\farcs1, and at a moon separation of 91\degree.
elements.sent-2411-088: The two template observations had exposure times of 291 s and 293 s, resulting in \snr of 215 per pixel at 5500 \AA\,for both.
elements.sent-2411-089: Triple-shot exposures of rapidly rotating B stars were taken with the iodine cell in the light path immediately before and after the template was collected to precisely constrain the instrumental PSF.
elements.sent-2411-090: The data collection and reduction followed the methods of the California Planet Search (CPS) as described in \cite{howard10 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: We note here that while the HIRES template observations were obtained at a relatively high airmass, we do not find any evidence that this affects our RV measurements.
elements.sent-2411-092: Upon visual inspection, the DSST shows no signs of poor-performing deconvolution, which would typically manifest itself in the form of ripple-like features in the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-093: Furthermore, none of the PFS fitting parameters are correlated with the measured RVs, indicating that the DSST itself is not introducing systematics into the RV measurements.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, the median RV precision for the HIRES data is 1.2 \ms, which is typical for CPS observations of stars of similar spectral type.
elements.sent-2411-095: RVs were determined following the procedures of \cite{howard10 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: As part of a forward model, the stellar spectrum was divided into about 700 pieces between $\sim$5000--6000 \AA, with each piece being 2 \AA\ in width.
elements.sent-2411-097: For each piece, the product of the DSST and the Fourier Transform Spectrograph iodine spectrum was convolved with the PSF to match the iodine-in observation.
elements.sent-2411-098: As one of the free parameters, an RV for each piece of spectrum was produced.
elements.sent-2411-099: The pieces were weighted using all observations of the star to produce a single RV for each iodine-in spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-100: Pointwise measurement uncertainties were estimated by taking the weighted standard deviation of the mean of the velocity measured from each of the 2 \AA-wide spectral chunks across all observations.
elements.sent-2411-101: In addition to computing RVs from each spectrum, we also measured S-value activity indicators, which track Ca II H and K emission strength.
elements.sent-2411-102: The S-values were computed following the methods of \citet{2024ApJ...961...85I .}

elements.sent-2411-217: The four-dimensional GP model is described by the following equations: \begin{equation}\Delta{\rm RV} = V_{\rm c} G(t) + V_{\rm r} \dot{G(t)} \log{R'_{\rm hk}} = L1_{\rm c} G(t) {\rm BIS} = B_{\rm c} G(t) + B_{\rm r} \dot{G(t)} {\rm H}\alpha = L2_{\rm c} G(t)\end{equation} where $G(t)$ and $\dot{G (t)$ are the underlying GP and its derivative, with the quasi-periodic kernel described by: \begin{equation}\gamma(t_i, t_j) = \exp{ \Biggl\{ - \frac{\sin^2{ \left[\pi (t_i -t_j)/ P_{\rm rot} \right] } }{2 w^2} - \frac{(t_i-t_j)^2}{2 P^2_{\rm dec}} \Biggl\} }\end{equation} where $P_{\rm rot}$ is the GP period equivalent to the stellar rotation period, $w$ is the coherence scale, and $P_{\rm dec}$ is associated with the decay time scale of the active regions.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\Delta{\rm RV} = V_{\rm c} G(t) + V_{\rm r} \dot{G(t)} \log{R'_{\rm hk}} = L1_{\rm c} G(t) {\rm BIS} = B_{\rm c} G(t) + B_{\rm r} \dot{G(t)} {\rm H}\alpha = L2_{\rm c} G(t)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\gamma(t_i, t_j) = \exp{ \Biggl\{ - \frac{\sin^2{ \left[\pi (t_i -t_j)/ P_{\rm rot} \right] } }{2 w^2} - \frac{(t_i-t_j)^2}{2 P^2_{\rm dec}} \Biggl\} }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-218: The constants $V_{\rm c}$, $V_{\rm r}$, $L1_{\rm c}$, $B_{\rm c}$, $B_{\rm r}$, and $L2_{\rm c}$ are free parameters that link the individual time series to the GP and its derivative (\citealt{2022A A...664A.163N,2023MNRAS.522.3458B ).}
